attributes.stem:
A 32-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with fatigue, headache, and body aches over the past few months. She also reports difficulty swallowing and progressive weakness in her arms and legs, making it hard to brush her hair and climb stairs. Her history includes a urinary tract infection caused by E. coli six months ago.

On examination, her blood pressure is 110/70 mmHg, pulse 80/min, temperature 37.7oC, and respiratory rate 18/min. A purplish rash is noted on her eyelids, and there is erythema on her face, neck, shoulders, and upper back, with scaly patches on her knuckles and elbows. Neurological examination reveals slightly decreased tone in both upper and lower limbs, power in both proximal arms and legs is 3/5, whereas power of the distal muscle group is 5/5. Plantars are down going and reflexes are normal. There are no fasciculations or muscle tenderness. The rest of the examination is unremarkable.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
The above clinical scenario is consistent with dermatomyositis which is a non-infectious, inflammatory disorder of skin and skeletal muscle characterised by bilateral symmetrical proximal muscle weakness, Gottron's papules (violaceous scaly patches symmetrically over knuckles, elbows and knees), heliotrope rash (purple-red eyelid discolouration with periorbital oedema), shawl sign (erythema of face, neck, shoulders, upper chest and back), dysphagia, interstitial lung disease, vasculitis and myocarditis.

Polymyositis causes similar symptoms to dermatomyositis but without the characteristic skin changes. Infectious myositis is a type of muscle inflammation that can be caused by a viral or bacterial infection. E. coli is a relatively rare cause. It can cause muscle weakness and pain but typically does not cause skin rashes. Since the patient has significant skin involvement, neither of these two options are correct.

Systemic sclerosis is an autoimmune disease that affects connective tissues thereby affecting skin, muscles, internal organs and vasculature. Skin involvement causes tightening and thickening of the skin, puffy hands, carpal tunnel syndrome, and purse string mouth. Gastrointestinal involvement may cause dysphagia, gastric outlet obstruction, bloating, diarrhoea etc. The patient does not have any of these symptoms so this is not the correct choice.

Motor neuron disease is a condition in which there is a progressive degeneration of the motor neurons. Symptoms include asymmetrical weakness of the distal muscle group in contrast to dermatomyositis in which there is symmetrical involvement of the proximal group of muscles. It is characterised by hyperreflexia, spasticity, positive Babinski's sign which is not seen in this patient.
learningPoints: Dermatomyositis is a systemic idiopathic inflammatory myopathy with distinctive cutaneous symptoms. The skin and muscles are usually involved frequently, although it can also affect joints, the oesophagus, the lungs, and, less frequently, the heart.

Patients with this disease usually present with cutaneous manifestations which include heliotrope rash, shawl sign, and Gottron papules. Muscle disease may occur simultaneously and cause symmetrical proximal muscle weakness, causing weakness or exhaustion when walking, getting up from a chair, combing hair, or overhead activity.
references: Qudsiya Z, Waseem M. Dermatomyositis. [Updated 2022 Aug 8]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2023 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK558917/

Marvi U, Chung L, Fiorentino DF. Clinical presentation and evaluation of dermatomyositis. Indian J Dermatol. 2012 Sep;57(5):375-81. doi: 10.4103/0019-5154.100486. PMID: 23112358; PMCID: PMC3482801. Available from: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3482801/

Patel D, Patel K, Parekh S, Ajwani T, Patel S. Beyond the Rash: Exploring the Symptoms and Diagnosis of Dermatomyositis. Cureus. 2023 Dec 5;15(12):e50011. doi: 10.7759/cureus.50011. PMID: 38207232; PMCID: PMC10769122. Available from: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10769122/
